Since the removal of the communist regime in 1993, laws  relating environmental protection haven passed. However,  these objectives are not binding and the government resources  are priotized on more pressing issues and prohibiting  widespread investment in such issues as protection of forest  such as Humbo. Good health and well being.summary: The Monitoring Report (Page 5) states that the project contributes to climate change mitigation objectives by creating Greenhouse gas (GHG) sinks through assisted natural regeneration of degraded lands. However, this evidence does not directly measure the number of farmers who have adopted alternative measures to prevent open burning of biomass and crop residue. The Verification report (Page 19) mentions that the project emissions for the actual monitoring period account to zero, implying that alternative measures may have been adopted to prevent open burning. However, this evidence is also indirect and does not provide explicit quantitative data on the number of farmers.

The evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable for these aspects, resulting in a conservative treatment. The score is 2 for the total number of employees who received skill development training.

Quality education.summary: The evidence from Document: CCIPL+1308_Final+Performance+Certification+Report+CC+19.09.2024(1).pdf, Page: 14, mentions training opportunities provided to employees, including induction training, employment contracts, DNHA assessment, plantation techniques, and training on endangered/native species. However, this evidence is qualitative and does not directly measure the indicator variable. The evidence from Document: 240814+ETH+Humbo+Monitoring+Report+(RP3)_FINAL(1).pdf, Page: 50, mentions a target of 20% women in managerial positions, but the actual number is reported as 13, which is 9.5% of the total positions. This evidence is also qualitative and does not directly measure the indicator variable. The evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement as it does not provide explicit quantitative data on the number of training hours provided for employees, disaggregated per gender.

5. Gender equality.summary: The evidence provided is weak and indirect, as it contains conflicting statements regarding the total number of persons employed in managerial positions and the proportion of females in managerial positions. The on-site inspection and interviews by VVB observed 13 women in managerial positions, which is 9.5% of the total positions in all 7 cooperatives (Document: CCIPL+1308_Final+Performance+Certification+Report+CC+19.09.2024.pdf, Page: 43). This contradicts the initial statement of 22% Female Managers = 28 female employees in management positions / 126 total employees (Document: 240814+ETH+Humbo+Monitoring+Report+(RP3)_FINAL(1).pdf, Page: 57). The evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable, and the numbers mentioned do not clearly support the indicator. Therefore, the evidence fails to satisfy the measurement requirement.

however no clear felling of project trees is allowed.

monitoring to ensure the forests is being managed in accordance with the 
conservation principles that the project is established on.

3.7.
Socio-Economic Impacts 
3.7.1.
Discussion
In order to maximize the community benefits of the project, a participatory approach was 
implemented.
Participatory rural appraisal (PRA) methods were adopted in interviewing and 
consulting with farmer households in the project areas to understand the local farmers/communities’ 
preferences, wishes and concerns, so that the proposed A/R CDM project activity would better

these goals: restoration of approximately 2728 ha of community land, community management, formation of 
community cooperative societies, monitoring system to monitor carbon stock changes and environmental 
and social issues.
The project is located in the Humbo Woreda, Wolayita zone.
Scope of verification 
This report summarizes the findings of the verification of the project, performed on the basis of UNFCCC

Ethiopia.
The project zone covers approximately 2724 hectares of land and includes 5 strata.
The project contributes to climate change mitigation objectives by creating Greenhouse gas 
(GHG) sinks through assisted natural regeneration of degraded lands.
Furthermore, the 
project compliments the natural resource management goals of the Ethiopian Agricultural 
Rural Development and Forestry Coordination Office (ARDFCO), and social development

project area.
(4) Contribution to sustainable development 
The project is natural regeneration project of using native species by FMNR and naturalized species.
The project promotes native vegetation and biodiversity.
Also, this project contributes to reduction in 
soil erosion and provision of income stream for communities.
(5) Duration of the project  
The beginning of crediting period is December 1, 2006.
The crediting period is defined as 30 years, fixed period.
3.2.2.
Findings

The project zone covers 2724 
hectares of land and includes 5 strata.
The project contributes to climate change 
mitigation objectives by creating Greenhouse gas (GHG) sinks through assisted 
natural regeneration of degraded lands.
Furthermore, the project compliments the 
natural resource management goals of the Ethiopian Agricultural Rural Development 
and Forestry Coordination Office (ARDFCO), and social development goals of the

Ethiopian government, and World Vision Ethiopia, the humanitarian organization 
implementing the project.
The Project has established seven community cooperative societies, which have legal 
ownership to 2,724 hectares of community land.
These groups are managing the 
areas using the Farmer Managed Natural Regeneration techniques for the purposes of 
carbon removal, environmental benefits (biodiversity, water quality, and habitat) and 
income producing activities for the local population.

goals of the Ethiopian government, and World Vision Ethiopia, the humanitarian organization 
implementing the project.
The Humbo Assisted regeneration project has established seven community cooperative 
societies, which have legal ownership to 2,724 hectares of community land.
These groups are 
managing the areas using the Farmer Managed Natural Regeneration techniques for the 
purposes of carbon removal, environmental benefits (biodiversity, water quality, and habitat)

and income producing activities for the local population.
Bylaws agreed upon at project 
inception form the rules for community cooperative societies to manage the project.
In summary, the implemented project activities are contributing to sustainable development in 
the following ways: 
1.
Regeneration of native forest, utilizing the farmer managed natural regeneration 
(FMNR) and traditional forest establishment techniques.
2.
Enhancement of GHG removals by sinks in the project area.
3.

Clean water and sanitation.summary: The evidence from the Humbo Monitoring Reports (240814+ETH+Humbo+Monitoring+Report+%28RP3%29_FINAL%281%29.pdf, Page: 69 and Humbo+Monitoring+Report+Period+20+December+2018_v7.pdf, Page: 3) suggests that the project's reforestation efforts have led to the regeneration of natural springs and a reduction in erosion, as reported by communities. However, the evidence does not provide explicit quantitative data on the capacity of water systems to retain and manage water resources sustainably. The mention of native vegetation and biodiversity improving the connectivity of fragmented forest resources and supporting the region's water supply is qualitative and does not directly measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

Decent work and economic growth.summary: The evidence from the employment census (Document: 240814+ETH+Humbo+Monitoring+Report+%28RP3%29_FINAL%281%29.pdf, Page: 58) and DNHA Assessment (Document: CCIPL+1308_Final+Performance+Certification+Report+CC+19.09.2024%281%29.pdf, Page: 17) does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Total Number of employees paid living wage'. Although the employment census includes data on job level, salary or wage, and years of employment, and the DNHA Assessment ensures compliance with labour laws, minimum wage, and working hours, no specific information is provided on the percentage of workers receiving a living wage. Therefore, the evidence is qualitative and does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

Decent work and economic growth.summary: The evidence from the Humbo Monitoring Report (RP3)_FINAL(1) and Humbo Monitoring Report Period 20 December 2018_v7 documents provides some information on income generation from forestry activities. The report mentions that local farmers participate in reforestation activities and that the cooperative societies engage in income-producing activities such as sustainable harvesting of forest resources. However, the evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Income generation from other activities for example ecotourism'. The mention of 195 jobs created is related to employment opportunities, not income generation from other activities. Therefore, the evidence is qualitative and does not directly measure the indicator variable.

Ethiopian government, and World Vision Ethiopia, the humanitarian organization 
implementing the project.
The Project has established seven community cooperative societies, which have legal 
ownership to 2,724 hectares of community land.
These groups are managing the 
areas using the Farmer Managed Natural Regeneration techniques for the purposes of 
carbon removal, environmental benefits (biodiversity, water quality, and habitat) and 
income producing activities for the local population.

3.
Promotion of native vegetation and biodiversity in the project area, which can be 
utilized as a refuge for local and migratory species and to improve the connectivity of 
fragmented forest resources.
4.
Reduction in soil erosion and flooding and help maintain supply of the subterranean 
streams to support the region’s water supply.
5.
Provision of an income stream for communities through sustainable harvesting of forest 
resources.

• 
Community management of public land with multiple objectives of promoting natural resource 
management, poverty alleviation and biodiversity enhancement 
• 
Development of a model of community land use that would enhance GHG removals by sinks 
from regenerating native vegetation, which can be replicable in other regions of Ethiopia.
•

Therefore, the evidence is qualitative only, and the score is 1.

The baseline net GHG removals by sinks are assumed to be constant, and monitoring of data and parameters related to fossil fuel and biomass burn is not required. 15. Life on land.summary: The evidence for the indicator 'Forest areas managed sustainably for forest products including sustainable produced fuelwood' is weak and indirect. The project has restored approximately 2728 hectares of biodiverse natural forest (Document: Validation+report+Rev+1+.pdf, Page: 6), but the exact total area under sustainable management practices is not specified. The project has implemented forest management activities such as pruning/thinning/coppice reduction (Document: Humbo+Monitoring+Report+Period+20+December+2018_v7.pdf, Page: 10) and farmer-managed natural regeneration techniques (Document: Humbo+Monitoring+Report+Period+20+December+2018_v7.pdf, Page: 6), but the percentage of forest areas certified under recognized sustainable forest management standards is unknown. The types of forest management practices being implemented include pruning, thinning, coppice reduction, and farmer-managed natural regeneration techniques (Document: Humbo+Monitoring+Report+Period+20+December+2018_v7.pdf, Page: 10). The key factors influencing the adoption of sustainable forest management practices include community participation, promotion of native vegetation and biodiversity, reduction in soil erosion and flooding, and provision of an income stream for communities through sustainable harvesting of forest resources (Document: Humbo+Monitoring+Report+Period+20+December+2018_v7.pdf, Page: 3).

As  identified  during  the  first  verification  /7/  minor  change  between  the  registered  project  and  the  implemented  project  were  identified;  the  changes were covered by the expectation of the Guidelines on accounting of specified types  of changes in A/R project activities form the description in registered PDD /8/ /43/.   Project implementation consisted of protection of natural regeneration in four strata (stratum  1, 2, 3, 4) and planting trees in stratum 5. In the initial planting in strata 5 around 500 ha out  of the project area were foreseen for supplementary planting, until today only 50.7 ha have  been planted with Eucalyptus globulus and Grevillea robusta. Planting have been concluded  in 2011.   Live fences have been established around the project boundary: along all the sites visited it  is  confirmed  that  the  live  fences  are  made  of  Agave  americana,  Euphorbia  tirucalli  and  Euphorbia scoparia.   As per the previous verification report the re-stratification has been carried out to ensure the  homogeneity of each stratum and to reduce variance in the statistical calculation approach; it  took  place  at  three  areas  in  the  project  boundary.  Parts  of  former  stratum  2  have  been  changed in stratum 3 and part of stratum 3 have been changed in stratum 4 /7/.   In  2015  was  built  a  road  through  strata  3  which  reduced  the  project  area  by  4.07  ha  deducted  from  strata  3  project  area.  The  construction  of  the  road  was  planned  by  the  Government  in  response  to  community’s  request.  The  estimated  overall  lost  carbon  stock  removal due to that is 823 tCO2e. In this regard the Government has proposed to replace the  amount  of  area  taken  by  the road  with protected  areas  (Moroto,  Hamessa  and  Kijiji) in the  vicinity  of  the  project  /19/.  During  the  interview  with  the  Head  of  Environment  Forestry  &  Climate Change Office, he confirmed that the area replaced accounts to 6.78 ha but actually  is not included in the project area.    No relevant forest or land use legislation has been changed since project start as confirmed  by the Head of Environment Forestry & Climate Change Office. The  specific  change  occurred  in  2015  with  the  road  construction  is  identified  as  minor  in  nature  as  per  the  Guidelines  on  accounting  of  specified  types  of  changes in A/R project activities form the description in registered PDD /22/.

Sample  plots  calculations  show  that  between  6.4  and  8.2t of fuel wood has been harvested per hectare per year, and  this  is  evenly  distributed  across  all  strata  and  species  /18/.  Annually  is  the  monitoring  frequency.  The  selected  cooperatives  are  Abela  Longena,  Bossa  Wanche,  Hobicha  Bada  and  Hobicha  Bongota  where  major  pruning  and  tinning  activities have been carried out. 15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided does not directly measure the total above and below ground biomass stock in forests within a specific geographic scope. Although allometric equations for Natural Regeneration, Eucalyptus, and Grevillea are given, they require necessary data such as diameter at breast height (DBH) or circumference at breast height (X) to calculate above-ground biomass. The equation to estimate carbon stock in below-ground biomass using root-shoot ratios and above-ground carbon stock is also provided, but no specific values for root-shoot ratios or above-ground carbon stock are given. The precision estimate for the 2021 forest carbon stock inventory was calculated as 14% at 90% confidence levels, but this does not directly measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the score is 0.

Life on land.summary: The evidence from the verification report (Document: GS+10220+-+Verification+report.+2018-1.pdf, Page: 12) provides explicit quantitative data that directly measures the indicator variable. The total area of land on which trees were planted in a given year is 50.7 ha. This evidence satisfies the indicator's measurement requirement as it directly measures the total area of trees planted. However, the evidence does not provide information on the time trend of tree planting efforts over a specified period, which is also a relevant aspect of the indicator.

The land eligibility was demonstrated by PRA which was carried out on 31st March to 2nd April, 2008 
and other field survey report.
According to the PRA report, before 1974 the Humbo forest was characterized by thick and dense 
forest with height greater than 7m. After 1975, due to the shift to communist government, the access 
to forest was made open to the public.

: Conclusion | forest  restoration  over  a  short  period.  As  identified  during  the  first  verification  /7/  minor  change  between  the  registered  project  and  the  implemented  project  were  identified;  the  changes were covered by the expectation of the Guidelines on accounting of specified types  of changes in A/R project activities form the description in registered PDD /8/ /43/.   Project implementation consisted of protection of natural regeneration in four strata (stratum  1, 2, 3, 4) and planting trees in stratum 5. In the initial planting in strata 5 around 500 ha out  of the project area were foreseen for supplementary planting, until today only 50.7 ha have  been planted with Eucalyptus globulus and Grevillea robusta. Planting have been concluded  in 2011.   Live fences have been established around the project boundary: along all the sites visited it  is  confirmed  that  the  live  fences  are  made  of  Agave  americana,  Euphorbia  tirucalli  and  Euphorbia scoparia.   As per the previous verification report the re-stratification has been carried out to ensure the  homogeneity of each stratum and to reduce variance in the statistical calculation approach; it  took  place  at  three  areas  in  the  project  boundary.  Parts  of  former  stratum  2  have  been  changed in stratum 3 and part of stratum 3 have been changed in stratum 4 /7/.   In  2015  was  built  a  road  through  strata  3  which  reduced  the  project  area  by  4.07  ha  deducted  from  strata  3  project  area.  The  construction  of  the  road  was  planned  by  the  Government  in  response  to  community’s  request.  The  estimated  overall  lost  carbon  stock  removal due to that is 823 tCO2e. In this regard the Government has proposed to replace the  amount  of  area  taken  by  the road  with protected  areas  (Moroto,  Hamessa  and  Kijiji) in the  vicinity  of  the  project  /19/.  During  the  interview  with  the  Head  of  Environment  Forestry  &  Climate Change Office, he confirmed that the area replaced accounts to 6.78 ha but actually  is not included in the project area.    No relevant forest or land use legislation has been changed since project start as confirmed  by the Head of Environment Forestry & Climate Change Office. In terms of land use rights  the  project  given  the  land  use  rights  officially  to  communities  as  confirmed  during  the  in  interview of Cooperatives leaders and members.: By  mean  of  the  on-site  inspection,  RINA  has  assessed  that  all  physical  features  of  the  registered CDM project activity specified in the registered PDD are in place and confirms that  the implementation and operation of the project activity has been conducted in accordance  with the description contained in the registered PDD and the minor change identified during  the  first  verification.    The  specific  change  occurred  in  2015  with  the  road  construction  is  identified  as  minor  in  nature  as  per  the  Guidelines  on  accounting  of  specified  types  of  changes in A/R project activities form the description in registered PDD /22/.

Brief description of the installed technology and equipment; 
 
The technology employed in this project is Farmer Managed Natural Regeneration (FMNR), 
and planting of seedlings raised from nursery stock.
The FMNR technique has been 
developed in Niger Republic, West Africa over 20 years where it is now practiced on over 2 
million hectares.
The technique has also spread to Chad, Burkina Faso and Senegal.

15. Life on land.summary: The evidence does not directly measure the total protected land area. Although the project area covers 2724 hectares of land, it is not clear how much of this area is designated as protected. The project participant response notes that determining and dedicating 10% of the project area as a conservation/protection area was found to be challenging. The minimum percentage of project area that must be protected to meet the indicator threshold is 10%, but the actual protected area is not explicitly stated. Therefore, the evidence is qualitative only, and the score is 1.

What is the total land area of the proje.content: 03    FAR ID  Section  Date: 13/2019  no.: While it is assumed the entirety of the project area is intended for the management and protection  of  biodiversity,  has  10%  of  the  project  area  specifically  been  designated  as  such,  and  being  managed using the HCV approach as required? The reviewer acknowledges the prohibited activities  in the required buffer zones do not appear to be taking place as part of the project activities. Given  the  number  of  years  the  project  has  operation  under  the  CDM  program,  and  the  reviewers  understanding  of  the  management  approach  being  taken,  the  requirement  to  implement  management and protection of 10% of the project area as a conservation/protection area using the  HCV approach can be assessed again during the next performance certification.

: Conclusion | forest  restoration  over  a  short  period.  As  identified  during  the  first  verification  /7/  minor  change  between  the  registered  project  and  the  implemented  project  were  identified;  the  changes were covered by the expectation of the Guidelines on accounting of specified types  of changes in A/R project activities form the description in registered PDD /8/ /43/.   Project implementation consisted of protection of natural regeneration in four strata (stratum  1, 2, 3, 4) and planting trees in stratum 5. In the initial planting in strata 5 around 500 ha out  of the project area were foreseen for supplementary planting, until today only 50.7 ha have  been planted with Eucalyptus globulus and Grevillea robusta. Planting have been concluded  in 2011.   Live fences have been established around the project boundary: along all the sites visited it  is  confirmed  that  the  live  fences  are  made  of  Agave  americana,  Euphorbia  tirucalli  and  Euphorbia scoparia.   As per the previous verification report the re-stratification has been carried out to ensure the  homogeneity of each stratum and to reduce variance in the statistical calculation approach; it  took  place  at  three  areas  in  the  project  boundary.  Parts  of  former  stratum  2  have  been  changed in stratum 3 and part of stratum 3 have been changed in stratum 4 /7/.   In  2015  was  built  a  road  through  strata  3  which  reduced  the  project  area  by  4.07  ha  deducted  from  strata  3  project  area.  The  construction  of  the  road  was  planned  by  the  Government  in  response  to  community’s  request.  The  estimated  overall  lost  carbon  stock  removal due to that is 823 tCO2e. In this regard the Government has proposed to replace the  amount  of  area  taken  by  the road  with protected  areas  (Moroto,  Hamessa  and  Kijiji) in the  vicinity  of  the  project  /19/.  During  the  interview  with  the  Head  of  Environment  Forestry  &  Climate Change Office, he confirmed that the area replaced accounts to 6.78 ha but actually  is not included in the project area.    No relevant forest or land use legislation has been changed since project start as confirmed  by the Head of Environment Forestry & Climate Change Office. In terms of land use rights  the  project  given  the  land  use  rights  officially  to  communities  as  confirmed  during  the  in  interview of Cooperatives leaders and members.: By  mean  of  the  on-site  inspection,  RINA  has  assessed  that  all  physical  features  of  the  registered CDM project activity specified in the registered PDD are in place and confirms that  the implementation and operation of the project activity has been conducted in accordance  with the description contained in the registered PDD and the minor change identified during  the  first  verification.    The  specific  change  occurred  in  2015  with  the  road  construction  is  identified  as  minor  in  nature  as  per  the  Guidelines  on  accounting  of  specified  types  of  changes in A/R project activities form the description in registered PDD /22/.

: Findings | forest  restoration  over  a  short  period.  As  identified  during  the  first  verification  /7/  minor  change  between  the  registered  project  and  the  implemented  project  were  identified;  the  changes were covered by the expectation of the Guidelines on accounting of specified types  of changes in A/R project activities form the description in registered PDD /8/ /43/.   Project implementation consisted of protection of natural regeneration in four strata (stratum  1, 2, 3, 4) and planting trees in stratum 5. In the initial planting in strata 5 around 500 ha out  of the project area were foreseen for supplementary planting, until today only 50.7 ha have  been planted with Eucalyptus globulus and Grevillea robusta. Planting have been concluded  in 2011.   Live fences have been established around the project boundary: along all the sites visited it  is  confirmed  that  the  live  fences  are  made  of  Agave  americana,  Euphorbia  tirucalli  and  Euphorbia scoparia. Project implementation consisted of protection of natural regeneration in four strata (stratum  1, 2, 3, 4) and planting trees in stratum 5. In the initial planting in strata 5 around 500 ha out  of the project area were foreseen for supplementary planting, until today only 50.7 ha have  been planted with Eucalyptus globulus and Grevillea robusta. Planting have been concluded  in 2011.   Live fences have been established around the project boundary: along all the sites visited it  is  confirmed  that  the  live  fences  are  made  of  Agave  americana,  Euphorbia  tirucalli  and  Euphorbia scoparia.   As per the previous verification report the re-stratification has been carried out to ensure the  homogeneity of each stratum and to reduce variance in the statistical calculation approach; it  took  place  at  three  areas  in  the  project  boundary.  Parts  of  former  stratum  2  have  been  changed in stratum 3 and part of stratum 3 have been changed in stratum 4 /7/.   In  2015  was  built  a  road  through  strata  3  which  reduced  the  project  area  by  4.07  ha  deducted  from  strata  3  project  area. 15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided in the Intermediate Answers and Context satisfies the indicator's measurement requirement. The total land area where native species were planted is explicitly stated as 2228 ha (Document: Validation+report+Rev+1+.pdf, Page: 8). However, the total number of native trees planted is not provided, which is necessary to calculate the percentage of native species planted in relation to the total number of species planted. Therefore, the score is 2 for the total land area where native species were planted, but 0 for the percentage of native species planted.

As for the species, native species are applied to 2228 ha among 2728 ha of total project area 
utilizing FMNR3 (Farmer Managed Natural Regeneration) technique and traditional forest 
establishment technique.
For other project area (500ha out of 2728 ha) naturalized species 
(Grevillea robusta and Eucalyptus globulus) are planted.
The annual rainfall of the project area is 700 – 1000mm and FMNR technique is suitable to this 
project area.

: Findings | forest  restoration  over  a  short  period.  As  identified  during  the  first  verification  /7/  minor  change  between  the  registered  project  and  the  implemented  project  were  identified;  the  changes were covered by the expectation of the Guidelines on accounting of specified types  of changes in A/R project activities form the description in registered PDD /8/ /43/.   Project implementation consisted of protection of natural regeneration in four strata (stratum  1, 2, 3, 4) and planting trees in stratum 5. In the initial planting in strata 5 around 500 ha out  of the project area were foreseen for supplementary planting, until today only 50.7 ha have  been planted with Eucalyptus globulus and Grevillea robusta. Planting have been concluded  in 2011.   Live fences have been established around the project boundary: along all the sites visited it  is  confirmed  that  the  live  fences  are  made  of  Agave  americana,  Euphorbia  tirucalli  and  Euphorbia scoparia.   As per the previous verification report the re-stratification has been carried out to ensure the  homogeneity of each stratum and to reduce variance in the statistical calculation approach; it  took  place  at  three  areas  in  the  project  boundary.  Parts  of  former  stratum  2  have  been  changed in stratum 3 and part of stratum 3 have been changed in stratum 4 /7/.   In  2015  was  built  a  road  through  strata  3  which  reduced  the  project  area  by  4.07  ha  deducted  from  strata  3  project  area.  The  construction  of  the  road  was  planned  by  the  Government  in  response  to  community’s  request.  The  estimated  overall  lost  carbon  stock  removal due to that is 823 tCO2e. In this regard the Government has proposed to replace the  amount  of  area  taken  by  the road  with protected  areas  (Moroto,  Hamessa  and  Kijiji) in the  vicinity  of  the  project  /19/.  During  the  interview  with  the  Head  of  Environment  Forestry  &  Climate Change Office, he confirmed that the area replaced accounts to 6.78 ha but actually  is not included in the project area.    No relevant forest or land use legislation has been changed since project start as confirmed  by the Head of Environment Forestry & Climate Change Office. In terms of land use rights  the  project  given  the  land  use  rights  officially  to  communities  as  confirmed  during  the  in  interview of Cooperatives leaders and members.: /

3.2.1 Discussion 
(1) General Description 
The proposed afforestation / reforestation activity, the Humbo Ethiopia Assisted Natural 
Regeneration project, involves the restoration of indigenous tree species in a mountainous region of 
South Western Ethiopia.
The project contributes to climate change mitigation objectives by 
contributing to the GHG removals by sinks through assisted natural regeneration project.
Report No. GR09W0008D: The project is natural regeneration project of using native species by FMNR and naturalized species.

Project implementation consisted of protection of natural regeneration in four strata (stratum  1, 2, 3, 4) and planting trees in stratum 5. In the initial planting in strata 5 around 500 ha out  of the project area were foreseen for supplementary planting, until today only 50.7 ha have  been planted with Eucalyptus globulus and Grevillea robusta. Planting have been concluded  in 2011.   Live fences have been established around the project boundary: along all the sites visited it  is  confirmed  that  the  live  fences  are  made  of  Agave  americana,  Euphorbia  tirucalli  and  Euphorbia scoparia.   As per the previous verification report the re-stratification has been carried out to ensure the  homogeneity of each stratum and to reduce variance in the statistical calculation approach; it  took  place  at  three  areas  in  the  project  boundary.  Parts  of  former  stratum  2  have  been  changed in stratum 3 and part of stratum 3 have been changed in stratum 4 /7/.   In  2015  was  built  a  road  through  strata  3  which  reduced  the  project  area  by  4.07  ha  deducted  from  strata  3  project  area.  The  construction  of  the  road  was  planned  by  the  Government  in  response  to  community’s  request.  The  estimated  overall  lost  carbon  stock  removal due to that is 823 tCO2e. In this regard the Government has proposed to replace the  amount  of  area  taken  by  the road  with protected  areas  (Moroto,  Hamessa  and  Kijiji) in the  vicinity  of  the  project  /19/.  During  the  interview  with  the  Head  of  Environment  Forestry  &  Climate Change Office, he confirmed that the area replaced accounts to 6.78 ha but actually  is not included in the project area.    No relevant forest or land use legislation has been changed since project start as confirmed  by the Head of Environment Forestry & Climate Change Office. In terms of land use rights  the  project  given  the  land  use  rights  officially  to  communities  as  confirmed  during  the  in  interview of Cooperatives leaders and members.: By  mean  of  the  on-site  inspection,  RINA  has  assessed  that  all  physical  features  of  the  registered CDM project activity specified in the registered PDD are in place and confirms that  the implementation and operation of the project activity has been conducted in accordance  with the description contained in the registered PDD and the minor change identified during  the  first  verification.    The  specific  change  occurred  in  2015  with  the  road  construction  is  identified  as  minor  in  nature  as  per  the  Guidelines  on  accounting  of  specified  types  of  changes in A/R project activities form the description in registered PDD /22/.
